The floodwaters are rising fast, and I must get my anvils and hammers to higher ground.
If the fire in my forge goes out from the rain, it will take hours to bring it back to heat.
This earthquake has cracked the stone foundation of my shop, and my heavy tongs are buried under rubble.
I can use the bellows to clear the ash and smoke if the wind shifts during this storm.
My iron stock is rusted from the dampness, so I will need to scrub every bar before heating.
That light in the sky burns with a hue I have never seen in my own forge.
I wonder if the heat from that falling star could soften a slab of cold iron.
The air feels heavy and charged, like the moment just before the hammer strikes the anvil.
If that celestial fire lands nearby, I will gather the metal it leaves behind for quenching.
My bellows are silent now as I watch the heavens crack open like a glowing ingot.
Your fence line is cutting right through the path I use to haul charcoal to the forge.
I cannot have your livestock trampling the clay pits where I dig the lining for my furnace.
Move that marker back three paces or you'll find no one to shoe your horses this winter.
The soot from my bellows drifts onto your land, but the wind puts it there, not me.
This strip of earth is where I stack my slag, and I'll not move it for a whim.
I wonder if the heat from that swirling void could soften a slab of high-carbon steel.
This shimmering gateway looks as though it were forged from a metal I have never seen.
If I step through, I hope there are ores in that realm that defy the hammer.
The air around this portal smells of ozone and hot slag, like a furnace gone wild.
I will keep my heavy tongs in hand in case this thing tries to pull me in.
These thick brambles are tearing at my leather apron and snagging my heavy tools.
I need to find a steady stream and some dry hardwood to build a forge.
My hammer is weighing me down, but I cannot leave my primary tools behind.
The damp air of this forest is going to rust my finest steel chisels.
I must search for an outcrop of iron ore before my current supplies run dry.
The forge has grown cold and the rhythmic ringing of my hammer is gone.
I wait in this heavy stillness, listening for the hiss of hot steel in water.
My tongs hang idle and the bellows stay slumped while the silence stretches on.
There is no clatter of armor or shouting of apprentices to break this quiet.
I stare at the charcoal embers, wondering when the next order will finally arrive.
Step back from my forge or you will find out how hot this iron truly is.
I have a heavy rounding hammer in my hand and I am not afraid to use it.
You will not lay a finger on my anvil while I still have breath in my lungs.
Get out of my shop before I pin you to the wall with a red-hot spike.
Try to take my tools and you will feel the weight of a sledgehammer on your ribs.
This anvil has a ring as clear as a bell and never cracks under the heaviest blows.
I spent three winters folding the steel of this blade to purge every single impurity from the grain.
No other pair of tongs in the shop can grip a glowing ingot with such steady precision.
The heat of this forge is the most consistent I have ever managed to maintain in my shop.
I keep this master hammer polished and balanced because it feels like an extension of my own arm.
I must husband these last few lumps of charcoal or the forge will go cold before the blade is tempered.
There is barely enough iron left in the scrap pile to finish this horse shoe without compromising the strength.
I cannot waste a single ounce of flux when the trade caravans are delayed and my stores are empty.
The bellows are worn thin, and I must be careful not to waste the heat on a poorly timed strike.
This piece of high-carbon steel is too precious to risk on a project that might fail in the quench.
